description: Full repair-card catalog: historical/veriloga-skills local cards plus Codex/system-relation cards.

cards.title: Drive Differential DAC Output From Decoded Code
text: Preserve the already-working code/input activity and repair only the analog output mechanism.
Decode the visible input/control bits into one integer code using fixed-index reads, then normalize that code to the output range.
Use a mid-rail logic threshold for public 0/VDD bit stimuli; do not set the threshold equal to the high rail when using strict `>` comparisons.
Update the decoded code and output target variables inside the same clock/event block; outside the event, keep only unconditional contributions from held target variables.
For differential outputs, compute a signal around common-mode and drive `out_p = vcm + signal/2`, `out_n = vcm - signal/2`.
Use real target variables and unconditional `V(out) <+ transition(target, ...)` contributions; do not put `transition()` inside branches.
For segmented DACs, combine thermometer/MSB contribution and binary/LSB contribution into one normalized code before driving the differential target.

cards.title: Quantize Input Into Output Code Bits
text: The input stimulus is already visible; focus on converting input voltage into changing output code bits.
Compute an integer code from the analog input, clamp it into `[0, 2^N - 1]`, and update it on the intended sample/clock event or continuously for combinational flash ADCs.
Drive each output bit from that integer code with fixed bit positions, using target variables and unconditional `transition()` contributions.
Check threshold direction and bit ordering: the checker-visible MSB/LSB names must match the saved column names.

cards.title: Keep ADC Quantization And DAC Reconstruction Coupled
text: This task needs both halves of the chain: input voltage should produce a digital code, and that same code should reconstruct an analog output.
Preserve the artifact boundary: keep existing file names, module names, ports, includes, saves, and the generated testbench unless the contract explicitly flags a TB failure.
First compute a clipped integer ADC code from `vin`; then drive the saved code bits from that integer.
Use the same integer code to compute the DAC output level, scaled across the public supply/reference range.
Do not rewrite the multi-file structure or interface while fixing behavior; keep `vout` and code-bit column names checker-visible.
Do not leave `vout` tied to a constant or to the raw input; the checker expects a quantized reconstruction that moves with code.

cards.title: Assert Bandgap Calibration Settled Only After Stable Cycles
text: Use an explore-freeze-settle calibration pattern: first let the trim code move, then explicitly freeze the final trim code, then count stable public clock cycles, then assert SETTLED and hold it high.
Treat passed `TRIM_code_changes` as an invariant: keep the existing trim update/search path and add only the stable-count/SETTLED mechanism unless the trim path is explicitly failing.
For `bg_cal`, a fixed-high `COMP_OUT` stimulus is allowed; do not try to make `COMP_OUT` move or derive success from comparator activity.
Do not keep searching forever. Once trim activity has covered the required range or the calibration error is within tolerance, enter a frozen/done phase and stop changing the trim code.
Count consecutive stable cycles at the public CLK cadence while the held trim code is unchanged; do not count only rare averaging/update events.
After the stable counter reaches the required consecutive-cycle count, drive SETTLED from a held flag and keep it high through the final validation window.
Keep one held integer trim_code as the source of truth for TRIM_0..TRIM_5, and drive SETTLED with an unconditional transition() contribution.

Avoid increasing averaging latency beyond the validation window. If the harness or anchor uses a public averaging parameter, preserve that parameter name and do not replace it with a slower private parameter.
Preserve the existing interface and testbench; drive the flag with an unconditional `transition()` contribution.

cards.title: Connect Calibration Code To Measured Response
text: The calibration/search code is already changing; repair the measured analog/comparator response that should depend on that code.
Model a trim effect from the decoded code, compare it against the target/input error, and update the comparator/measurement output from that comparison.
For CDAC-style calibration, decode the calibration bits into a weighted DAC value before driving `vdac_p/vdac_n` or the comparator input.
Hold the final code/output state after the search completes; do not keep outputs disconnected from the calibration state.

cards.title: Build Segmented DAC Differential Output From Weighted Segments
text: Preserve the existing segmented-DAC interface and testbench; repair only the DUT summation/output path.
Decode `D*` bits with a mid-supply threshold so public 0/0.9 V pulses are sampled as real logic highs; a `vth` equal to 0.9 V with `>` will read every high bit as 0.
Decode thermometer/MSB and binary/LSB controls separately, then sum them with the correct segment weights into one normalized code.
Compute `vout_diff`, `vout_p_target`, and `vout_n_target` immediately after decoding the code in the clock event; do not leave target computation as free procedural statements after the event body.
Compute a differential signal from that weighted sum around common-mode and drive both output pins with opposite polarity.
Use held real targets plus unconditional `V(out_p/out_n) <+ transition(target, ...)`; do not leave either side constant.

cards.title: Toggle Divider Output From Input Clock Counter
text: The input clock is present; repair the divider state machine rather than the testbench clock.
On each input rising edge, increment a counter. When it reaches the active division ratio, reset the counter and toggle the output state.
If the ratio changes at runtime, latch or read the ratio code at a safe clock edge and apply it to subsequent counter periods.
Drive the output from a held integer state with an unconditional `transition()` contribution.

cards.title: Repair PFD Edge-Pair Pulse Symmetry
text: Preserve the public PFD parameter interface. If the harness passes `vth` and `tedge`, the repaired module must declare both parameters and should use `tedge` in output transitions.
Treat REF-leading and DIV-leading edge pairs symmetrically: REF rising sets a held UP state, DIV rising sets a held DN state.
Do not suppress the second side by requiring both states to be zero before setting a latch. A trailing edge should either clear an existing opposite latch or create its own response when it leads.
Use independent held `up_state` and `dn_state` variables plus a small delayed-clear or finite pulse timer so pulses are visible but do not stay high.
Keep `@(cross(...))` event statements unconditional in the analog block; put reset, set, and clear decisions inside event bodies.
Drive `up` and `dn` from held target variables with unconditional `transition()` contributions. Do not place `transition()` inside `if` branches.
For reset-race tasks, preserve the already-passing non-overlap behavior while adding the missing response side. For deadzone tasks, keep the non-leading side mostly low.

cards.title: Use Symmetric PFD Latches For UP/DN Pulses
text: Preserve the public PFD parameter interface. If the harness passes `vth` and `tedge`, the repaired module must declare both parameters and should use `tedge` in output transitions.
A PFD should have paired latch behavior: REF edge sets UP, DIV edge sets DN.
When both latches are high, clear both after a small delay so pulses are finite and non-overlapping.
If only one side pulses, check that both edge event handlers update independent held states and that both outputs are driven from those states.
Preserve module ports, node names, and the existing testbench/interface while repairing the latch logic.
Do not gate or conditionally instantiate `@(cross())` statements; put reset/clear conditions inside event bodies.
Do not put `transition()` calls inside conditional branches; compute held UP/DN target states first, then drive outputs unconditionally.

cards.title: Schedule Absolute Timer Events And Toggle A Held Output
text: Use a held next-event time variable such as `t_next`, initialized in `@(initial_step)`.
Schedule `@(timer(t_next))`, toggle a held output state, then advance `t_next` by the required interval.
Drive the saved output from the held state with an unconditional `transition()` contribution.
Avoid two-argument periodic timers when the next interval must align to an absolute grid.

cards.title: Close PLL Feedback Edge And Lock Path
text: Preserve the public REF/FB/control/lock interface and the existing testbench stimulus.
Repair the edge path before tuning lock thresholds: reference activity plus zero feedback edges means the oscillator/divider feedback generator is not alive.
Use a held oscillator period or frequency state and schedule every next output edge strictly in the future.
Tie feedback divider toggling to generated oscillator edges; do not derive feedback from a one-shot initialization event.
Update control voltage or control monitor from phase/frequency error so it moves when the loop is unlocked.
Assert lock only after feedback/reference cadence is close for a stable window; do not force lock high while the ratio contract is failing.

cards.title: Stage 1 PLL Repair: Make The Feedback Clock Alive
text: Treat missing `fb_clk` edges as a stage-1 PLL failure. Do not start by changing lock thresholds; first create a real feedback clock path.

Preserve every public verifier parameter name that the testbench passes. For ADPLL/CPPLL tasks this commonly includes `div_ratio`, `f_center`, `freq_step_hz`, `f_min`, `f_max`, `code_min`, `code_max`, `code_center`, `code_init`, `tedge`, `lock_tol`, and `lock_count_target`; ratio-hop tasks may also pass `ratio_min` and `ratio_max`.
If you prefer internal names such as `ndiv`, `tdco_nom`, or `lock_thr`, derive them from the public parameters inside the analog code, but still declare the public parameters exactly.

Use one held oscillator/DCO state and one absolute `t_next` timer. Initialize `t_next` in `@(initial_step)`, toggle the oscillator state in `@(timer(t_next))`, then advance `t_next` by a strictly positive half-period.

For ADPLL-style tasks without a separate `dco_clk`, `fb_clk` may be the divided/toggled DCO feedback output. For CPPLL-style tasks with `dco_clk`, generate `dco_clk` first and derive `fb_clk` from divider/counter state.
Never schedule a future edge from a stale one-shot initialization event. Every generated edge must schedule the next edge explicitly in the future.
Preserve the public `ref_clk`, `fb_clk`, `vctrl_mon`, `lock`, supply, and parameter interface. Drive clock outputs from held states with unconditional `transition()` contributions.
Clamp oscillator frequency or period to a safe positive range so the timer cannot stop, go negative, or schedule an infinite-rate loop.

cards.title: Stage 2 PLL Repair: Match Feedback/Reference Ratio
text: Feedback edges already exist; preserve that liveness and repair the divider/control cadence that sets the feedback/reference ratio.

Preserve public verifier parameter names exactly. Do not replace `div_ratio`, `ratio_min`, `ratio_max`, `f_center`, `freq_step_hz`, `f_min`, `f_max`, `code_min`, `code_max`, `code_center`, `code_init`, `tedge`, `lock_tol`, or `lock_count_target` with private-only names.
Keep an integer divider counter connected to oscillator or DCO edges. Toggle or pulse `fb_clk` only from that counter, not from an unrelated timer.

Be explicit about half-period versus full-period counting. If the DCO timer fires on every half-cycle toggle, and `fb_clk` toggles after `N` DCO timer fires, then one full `fb_clk` period is `2*N` timer intervals. Choose `N` or the DCO period so the late-window `fb_clk` rising-edge rate matches `ref_clk`, not 20-30% high or low.

For ADPLL-style divided feedback, use the mechanism relation `f_dco ~= 2 * div_ratio * f_ref` when `fb_clk` toggles once per divider count. Changing `div_ratio` without a matching DCO nominal frequency will push the late-window edge ratio high or low.

For ADPLL verifier harnesses with a 50 MHz reference and `div_ratio=8`, a nominal DCO around `f_center` should be divided so `fb_clk` has a near-20 ns rising-edge period. Do not ignore `f_center` or replace it with a fixed private oscillator.
If the prompt exposes a ratio word, hop control, or mode input, latch it on a safe public clock edge and apply it to subsequent divider periods.
For a ratio-near-one checker, set the nominal feedback cadence so late-window `fb_clk` and `ref_clk` edge counts align before asserting lock.
Do not make `lock` pass by forcing it high while the ratio contract is failing. Ratio first, lock second.
When repairing CPPLL tasks, keep `dco_clk` alive and adjust the divider/control path rather than replacing the whole loop with an independent `fb_clk` generator.

cards.title: Stage 3 PLL Repair: Move Control From Phase/Frequency Error
text: `vctrl_mon` or the control monitor should be a held loop state, not a constant cosmetic output.

Keep the verifier parameter interface intact while adding control movement. The public harness may pass `f_center`, `freq_step_hz`, `f_min`, `f_max`, `code_min`, `code_max`, `code_center`, `code_init`, `ratio_min`, `ratio_max`, `tedge`, `lock_tol`, and `lock_count_target`; declare those names even if some are only used to clamp the behavioral model.

Update the control state from phase/frequency error: REF-leading events should move the control in the direction that speeds up feedback, and FB-leading events should move it in the direction that slows feedback.
A lightweight behavioral loop filter is acceptable: accumulate bounded UP/DN corrections into one real control variable, clamp it to supply/reference limits, and drive `vctrl_mon` from that variable.
Tie oscillator/DCO frequency to the same control variable so the monitor movement is causally connected to feedback cadence.
For ratio-hop or frequency-step tasks, preserve already-passing feedback edges and ratio behavior while adding visible control movement around the hop/step window.
Drive `vctrl_mon` with an unconditional contribution from the held control state; do not leave it at a fixed mid-rail value.

cards.title: Stage 4 PLL Repair: Assert Lock Only After Stable Ratio
text: Only repair lock after feedback edges and ratio are already plausible.
Preserve public lock and edge parameters such as `tedge`, `lock_tol`, and `lock_count_target`; use them to set transition time, allowed phase/frequency error, and required stable-window count.

Maintain a small stability counter over consecutive reference edges where recent reference and feedback cadence are close. A code-near-center test is not enough; the lock flag should summarize observed ref/fb timing stability.
Use `lock_tol` as a time or period tolerance when comparing recent ref/fb edge timing, and `lock_count_target` as the required number of consecutive stable observations.
`lock_count_target` mainly controls lock assertion latency; it should not be used to hide an incorrect feedback/reference ratio.
Assert a held `lock` flag only after the stability counter reaches the required window; clear or lower it when a hop, reset, or large frequency error occurs.
For reacquire tasks, allow lock to drop after the disturbance and assert again only after the post-step ratio has restabilized.
Do not use `lock = 1` as a shortcut. Lock must summarize a stable feedback path, not replace it.

cards.title: Drive DWA Pointer And Cell Enables From A Wrapped State
text: Preserve the public pointer/cell-enable/output column names and repair the state update path first.
Keep one integer pointer as the source of truth. Initialize it after reset and update it once per valid clock/sample event.
Decode the requested active cell count from the public input code, clamp it to the cell array size, and wrap selections modulo the number of cells.
Drive every saved `cell_en_*` and `ptr_*` bit from held integer state variables with unconditional voltage contributions.
For no-overlap tasks, ensure no cell enable is driven by two independent branches in the same timestep; compute the full mask first, then drive pins.

cards.title: Generate Flash ADC Code Coverage From Input Thresholds
text: If the input span contract passes but output code coverage fails, repair the quantizer/code path rather than the stimulus.
Compute a clipped integer code from the public analog input using evenly spaced thresholds across the public input/reference range.
For flash-style tasks, combinational threshold decoding is acceptable unless the prompt explicitly requires clocked sampling.
Drive each `dout_*` bit from the same integer code with the checker-visible bit order.
Use mid-supply digital thresholds for any enable/reset pins and keep output transitions unconditional.

cards.title: Recover Runtime CSV And Observable Interface
text: Repair the simulation artifact before changing DUT semantics.
Check that every required generated file exists with the expected filename and that the testbench includes that exact file.
Preserve module names, port order, discipline declarations, and save signal names from the public task contract.
Ensure the transient analysis is present, finite, and long enough for the required observables to be saved.
If a checker times out on a large CSV, prefer a streaming/lightweight checker path or a smaller save set before behavior tuning.

cards.title: Drive Event Outputs From Detected Crossings
text: Preserve the input waveform and repair the event-detection state that produces checker-visible outputs.
Use unconditional `@(cross(...))` or `@(above(...))` event statements in the analog block; put state updates inside event bodies.
Maintain held counters or error accumulators as real/integer variables and drive saved outputs from those held variables.
Do not compute saved outputs only inside the event branch; keep the voltage contributions unconditional so CSV columns move after events.
If the checker observes a count output, increment it exactly once per intended crossing and avoid reset conditions that clear it before the validation window.

cards.title: Keep SAR Sequence And Internal DAC Activity Coupled
text: A SAR task needs visible trial-code activity, not only a final held code.
Keep one SAR state machine with a bit index, trial code, comparator decision, and held final code.
On each valid sample/clock step, update the trial bit and internal DAC/code monitor before advancing to the next bit.
Drive public SAR/DAC monitor bits from the same trial-code integer so code coverage appears in the waveform.
After conversion completes, freeze the final code until the next sample event instead of continuing to toggle randomly.

cards.title: Map Thermometer-Code Population Count To Output Voltage
text: For thermometer-coded DACs, decode the output level from the number of asserted public input bits, not from a binary interpretation of bit positions.

Preserve active-low reset semantics: when reset is asserted low, force the held output target to the low rail or zero level; after reset release, keep the reset stimulus deasserted through the checking window.
Use a mid-supply threshold to count high input cells and compute `vout_target = count_of_ones * vstep` or the prompt-specified full-scale mapping.
Drive `vout` from one held real target with an unconditional `transition()` contribution.
Keep scalar save aliases such as `d0..d15` visible when the public prompt names them, even if the DUT internally uses a bus.

cards.title: Update Binary Count Then Encode One-Bit Gray Transitions
text: Keep a held binary counter as the source of truth and update it once per valid clock edge after reset release.
Encode the public outputs as `gray = binary ^ (binary >> 1)`; do not increment the Gray word directly.
Reset should drive both the binary counter and the Gray outputs to zero, then remain released during the checking window.
Drive every saved Gray bit from the held encoded word with unconditional voltage contributions.
If code coverage passes but one-bit transitions fail, preserve clock/reset stimulus and repair only the encoding/update order.

cards.title: Align Serializer Load, First Bit, And Frame Pulse
text: Treat serializer repair as a state-machine alignment problem, not as a free-running pulse generator.
Latch the parallel word only on the prompt-specified load event or load-qualified clock edge.
After loading, shift exactly one bit per clock edge in the requested order, commonly MSB-first when the prompt says so.
Assert the frame marker only for the first serialized bit of each loaded word, then clear it on later bits.
Drive `sout` and `frame` from held state variables with unconditional `transition()` contributions so sampling after clock edges is stable.

cards.title: Generate Pulse Train From Public Instance Parameters
text: Declare the public real and integer parameters exactly as the prompt names them so Spectre instance overrides bind to the DUT.
Use defaults that are not equal to the final test values when the prompt asks for override sensitivity.
Schedule a finite sequence with `@(timer(t_next))`: toggle or assert a held output state, count emitted pulses, and stop after the overridden repetition count.
Scale the high target from the overridden amplitude parameter, not from a hard-coded constant.
Drive the output from a held real target with an unconditional `transition()` contribution.

cards.title: Preserve Basic Logic Truth Tables And Harness Port Order
text: For basic logic tasks, first preserve the public module names, positional port order, include file names, and scalar save names from the prompt.
Implement combinational gates as direct thresholded logic functions using the public VDD/VSS rails or prompt-specified logic levels.
For sequential cells, sample only on the requested clock edge and treat reset polarity exactly as stated in the prompt.
Drive each output continuously from held or directly computed logic targets with `transition()`; do not hide outputs behind instance-qualified save names.
If the failure is CSV missing or checker timeout, fix the harness/save set before changing truth-table behavior.

cards.title: Detect Data/Clock Lead-Lag And Emit Non-Overlapping BBPD Pulses
text: For bang-bang/data-edge phase detectors, compare recent data-edge time against the nearby clock-edge time; do not treat data as a static logic input.
When the data edge leads the clock edge within the public checking window, emit a bounded UP pulse; when it lags, emit a bounded DN pulse.
Keep independent held UP and DN states and clear each pulse after a finite duration so the two outputs remain mostly non-overlapping.
Retimed data should update on clock edges from the sampled data level, while UP/DN pulses summarize the data/clock timing relationship.
Use top-level unconditional `@(cross())` event statements and drive UP/DN/retimed outputs from held targets with unconditional `transition()` contributions.

cards.title: Preserve The PLL Feedback-Control-Lock Relation Graph
text: Repair PLL tasks as one coupled system: reference edges, DCO/oscillator edges, feedback divider edges, control movement, and lock assertion must all describe the same loop.
Preserve the public parameter and port interface exactly; derive private helper names from public parameters instead of replacing them.
Generate one oscillator/DCO cadence state, derive feedback from a divider/counter on that cadence, and compare feedback against reference edges.
Update the control monitor from phase/frequency error and tie oscillator cadence to that same control state; do not make `vctrl_mon` a cosmetic moving signal.
Assert lock only after the late-window reference/feedback ratio is stable; on ratio hop or frequency step, allow lock to drop and reacquire after the new relation settles.
Avoid independent shortcuts such as a free-running feedback timer plus forced lock. Those can satisfy one observable while breaking the system relation.

cards.title: Preserve The DWA Pointer-Window-Enable Relation Graph
text: Treat DWA as a state relation between sampled input code, pointer state, selected cell window, and scalar observable bits.
Keep one integer pointer as the only source of truth. Initialize it from reset/parameter state, then update it once per valid clock edge.
Decode the sampled input code into an active-cell count, clamp it to the cell array size, and form the complete cell-enable mask before driving any output.
For wraparound, split the contiguous window across the array boundary using modulo arithmetic. For no-overlap, advance the pointer so consecutive selected sets do not reuse cells.
Drive `ptr_*` as a one-hot view of the held pointer and `cell_en_*` as the held selected mask; expose scalar nodes with stable names for every bit.
Do not repair DWA by only changing save aliases or by independently toggling cell enables. The pointer, active count, wrap behavior, and no-overlap rule must stay coupled.

cards.title: Preserve The Edge-Order To UP/DN Pulse Relation
text: Repair PFD/BBPD tasks by preserving the mapping from two input edge streams to finite UP/DN pulses.
Record recent edge times or latch states for both sides. The leading side determines which pulse is emitted; the trailing side clears or bounds the response.
Keep UP and DN as independent held states, then clear them after a finite delay so pulses are visible but mostly non-overlapping.
For BBPD, data is an edge stream relative to the clock, not a static logic input. Retimed data and UP/DN pulses should come from the same sampled edge-order relation.
Keep all `@(cross())` event controls unconditional in the analog block and drive outputs with unconditional `transition()` contributions from held states.

cards.title: Preserve The ADC Code And DAC Reconstruction Relation
text: Treat converter repairs as a chain: sample/reset state, quantized code, internal trial/control code, and analog reconstruction must agree.
If code coverage fails, repair sampling/reset/code update first. If code coverage passes but output span fails, preserve the code path and repair code-to-output decoding.
Use one held integer code or trial code as the source of truth for all public code-bit outputs and DAC/reconstruction targets.
For SAR/CDAC/calibration cases, make the trial/control bits move during the sequence, then freeze the final state and assert ready/settled only after completion.
For differential DAC outputs, compute one differential signal from the decoded code and drive positive/negative outputs with opposite polarity around common mode.
Do not make `vout` track raw `vin` continuously when the prompt asks for quantized reconstruction; the analog output should follow the same code that drives the bits.

cards.title: Preserve The Serializer Load-Shift-Frame Relation
text: Treat serializer tasks as a state machine, not as independent toggles on `sout` and `frame`.
Latch the parallel input word only on the specified load event, then shift exactly one bit per valid clock edge.
Keep a bit index and a held shift register as the source of truth. The serialized output should come from the current index before the index advances.
Assert the frame marker only for the first serialized bit of each loaded word, then clear it for the remaining bits.
Preserve scalar observable names and drive `sout`/`frame` from held targets with unconditional `transition()` contributions.
